description: State-by-state expungement and record sealing eligibility: waiting periods, Clean Slate automation, petition requirements, and disqualifying offenses for CA, TX, NY, FL, PA, IL, OH, MI, GA, NC.
sources: Clean Slate Initiative state tracker (cleanslateworks.org), verified 2025
National Inventory of Collateral Consequences of Conviction (NICCC), ABA

State legislative codes: CA Penal Code 1203.4, TX Code Crim Proc Ch. 55, NY CPL Article 160, FL Stat. 943.0585, PA 18 Pa.C.S. 9122, 730 ILCS 5/5-5.5, Ohio RC 2953.32, MCL 780.621, OCGA 35-3-37, NC GS 15A-145

triples.subject: expungement
triples.object: court order sealing or destroying criminal record; legal effect varies by state — some allow denial of arrest, others only seal from public view

triples.subject: Clean Slate states
triples.object: automatic expungement without petition; as of 2025: PA, MI, MN, CO, CT, DE, MD, NJ, NM, OK, UT, VA — no lawyer required

triples.subject: California (CA)
triples.object: Penal Code 1203.4 allows dismissal of conviction after completing probation; does not seal record but can deny on most private employment applications

triples.subject: California (CA)
triples.object: no waiting period after probation completion; automatic relief under AB 1076 (2023) for eligible felonies and misdemeanors — courts must act without petition

triples.subject: California (CA)
triples.object: serious felonies (PC 1192.7), violent felonies (PC 667.5b), sex offenses requiring registration, and child molestation are ineligible

triples.subject: California (CA)
triples.object: Certificate of Rehabilitation available after 7–10 years for felonies; provides evidence of rehabilitation for licensing boards

triples.subject: Texas (TX)
triples.object: Expunction under CCP Ch. 55 destroys records; available for arrests not leading to conviction, deferred adjudication for some Class A/B misdemeanors, and acquittals

triples.subject: Texas (TX)
triples.object: seals record from public but not from law enforcement; available for deferred adjudication probation after waiting period

triples.subject: Texas (TX)
triples.object: misdemeanors: 2 years after deferred adjudication discharge; felonies: 5 years after discharge — some felonies ineligible entirely

triples.subject: Texas (TX)
triples.object: family violence, stalking, trafficking, sexual offenses, murder, and most felonies involving firearms are ineligible for nondisclosure

triples.subject: New York (NY)
triples.object: CPL 160.59 allows sealing (not destruction) of up to 2 convictions (max 1 felony) after 10-year wait from sentence completion

triples.subject: New York (NY)
triples.object: 10 years from sentence OR release from incarceration, whichever is later

triples.subject: New York (NY)
triples.object: MRTA (2021) automatically expunges convictions for marijuana possession up to 3 oz; no petition needed

triples.subject: New York (NY)
triples.object: sex offenses, violent felonies under CPL 70.02, and Class A felonies are ineligible for sealing

triples.subject: Florida (FL)
triples.object: FL Stat. 943.0585 seals or expunges; sealing for first-time offenders who completed diversion; expungement after 10 years of sealing — one expungement per lifetime

triples.subject: Florida (FL)
triples.object: 10 years sealed before expungement; must obtain Certificate of Eligibility from FDLE before petitioning court

triples.subject: Florida (FL)
triples.object: very broad list: domestic violence, sexual offenses, child abuse, DUI with injury, robbery, carjacking, stalking, and others are ineligible for sealing or expungement

triples.subject: Pennsylvania (PA)
triples.object: Clean Slate Act (2018, expanded 2020) provides automatic sealing after 10 years for misdemeanors with sentences under 2 years and non-conviction records

triples.subject: Pennsylvania (PA)
triples.object: full expungement by petition for: summary offenses after 5 years, ARD completion, acquittals, charges nolle prossed, and age 70+ with 10 clean years

triples.subject: Pennsylvania (PA)
triples.object: felonies, M1 misdemeanors (unless older than 7 years with clean record), sex offenses, and offenses with sentences over 2 years not eligible for automatic sealing

triples.subject: Illinois (IL)
triples.object: 730 ILCS 5/5-5.5 allows expungement of arrests without conviction and some juvenile records; sealing available for broader set of adult convictions

triples.subject: Illinois (IL)
triples.object: misdemeanors: 3 years from sentence completion; most felonies: 5 years; Class 2–4 felonies eligible for sealing after 5 years under recent amendments

triples.subject: Illinois (IL)
triples.object: Cannabis Regulation Act (2019) provides automatic expungement for convictions of 30g or less possession — no action needed by individual

triples.subject: Ohio (OH)
triples.object: RC 2953.32 allows sealing of record; 2023 S.B. 288 greatly expanded eligibility — most misdemeanors and many felonies now eligible after waiting period

triples.subject: Ohio (OH)
triples.object: misdemeanors: 1 year; F4/F5 felonies: 3 years; F3 felonies: 5 years; F1/F2 felonies: 7 years from final discharge

triples.subject: Michigan (MI)
triples.object: Clean Slate Act (2021) provides automatic expungement after 7 years for misdemeanors (after 10 years for felonies) — applies to most offenses automatically

triples.subject: Michigan (MI)
triples.object: petition available immediately for some offenses; up to 3 felonies and unlimited misdemeanors can be set aside by petition under MCL 780.621

triples.subject: Georgia (GA)
triples.object: OCGA 35-3-37 allows restriction (sealing) of records for: non-conviction arrests, charges dismissed before indictment, and some first-offender completions

triples.subject: Georgia (GA)
triples.object: GA does not expunge convictions generally; First Offender Act (OCGA 42-8-60) avoids a conviction entirely if completed — must be applied at sentencing, not retroactively

triples.subject: North Carolina (NC)
triples.object: GS 15A-145 series allows expungement for non-convictions, some first-offense misdemeanors and felonies, and older convictions; 2017 Second Chance Act expanded eligibility

triples.subject: North Carolina (NC)
triples.object: non-violent misdemeanors: 5 years; non-violent Class H/I felonies: 10 years from sentence completion; some offenses have no waiting period if not convicted

triples.subject: petition-based expungement
triples.object: file petition in sentencing court → serve DA → attend hearing (sometimes waived) → judge reviews and signs order → clerk notifies arresting agencies and state repository

triples.subject: certificates of rehabilitation
triples.object: CA, IL, NM, and a few other states; not an expungement but signals rehabilitation to licensing boards; some states allow judicial certificates for employment purposes

triples.subject: federal convictions
triples.object: no general federal expungement statute for adult convictions; limited expungement available for some juvenile federal offenses and certain drug offenses under 18 USC 3607
